raw_case:
Fibroadipose Vascular Anomaly [FAVA] – A Distinct Entity and Not Just a Malformation!


Abstract



Introduction:

Fibroadipose vascular anomaly (FAVA) was described in 2014 as a distinct entity characterised by intramuscular replacement with fibro fatty tissue along with complex vascular malformation, phelbectesia, venous thrombosis and lymphatic involvement. Somatic mutations in the PIK3CA gene are detected in most lesions which diagnosed the FAVA in our report and occurrence of this mutation seems to be sporadic.

Case Report:

Common presentation is a painful intramuscular swelling in young women – as was the presentation here in an 11 year girl with the swelling of the right thigh. Imaging features, phleboliths and long standing history of an intramuscular malformation in the young girl that was recalcitrant to treatment at previous attempts led us towards the suspicion of a fibro adipose vascular anomaly.

Conclusion:

Surgery with en-bloc mass excision is recommended for good long term curative option for reducing pain and regaining movements. FAVA is a rare, but specific vascular anomaly that is often misdiagnosed with other intramuscular vascular malformations and therefore poses significant management challenges. It is imperative that clinicians have a thorough understanding of FAVA in order to provide proper diagnosis and treatment referrals.

Learning Point of the Article:

FAVA is a rare, but specific vascular anomaly that is often misdiagnosed with other intramuscular vascular malformations and therefore poses significant management challenges. It is imperative that clinicians have a thorough understanding of FAVA in order to provide proper diagnosis and treatment referrals.

Introduction

The recent description of a distinct entity in fibroadipose vascular anomaly (FAVA) by the International Society for the Study of Vascular Anomalies (ISSVA) in 2014 [1] and revised classification in 2018 [2, 3] brings to light the complex vascular anomalous malformation occurring within the muscles of young women (female-to-male predisposition described as 3:1) [4]. The sporadically occurring somatic mutations in the PIK3CA gene have been associated with most of these lesions [5]. We aim to describe the clinical, radiological as well as pathology features of our patient, our methodology toward the diagnosis and treatment for FAVA, and how it all differed from its close and common differential in a slow flow vascular malformation.

Case Report

We present an 11-year-old girl with a painful anterior right thigh swelling for 4 years. It began as a painful small lump deep in her anterior mid-thigh region and gradually progressed to a large swelling that caused her severe local pain and limitation of movements at the knee. Her mother noticed a limp with pelvis and shoulders dropping to the affected side while walking and this has progressed over the past year. She had undergone an incisional biopsy which was inconclusive and treated with intra-lesional chemical/sclerosant injection before presentation to our institution. Our clinical evaluation brought to light a deep intramuscular soft-tissue neoplasm in the anterior thigh muscles which was tender on palpation and was limiting her knee mobility with range 10°–40° possible on active and passive examination (Fig. 1). Hypotrophy of calf muscles recorded on girth circumference and limb length discrepancy from >2 cm of limb shortening on the affected side was recorded (Fig. 2).High-resolution ultrasound showed evidence of an ill-defined hyperechoic area within the muscles in the anterior compartment of thigh with few tubular vascular channels within on-power Doppler ultrasound, mostly representing a vascular malformation.A multiplanar multiecho plain and contrast magnetic resonance (MR) imaging of the right has been performed. In addition, MR angiogram of the right has been performed with arterial, venous, and delayed phases of scanning that reported a large heterogeneous intramuscular lesion in the anterior compartment of the right thigh predominantly involving the vastus muscles and sparing the rectus femoris with superoinferior length of approximately 24 cm. Regions of fat infiltration in relation to the muscles may represent the presence of fat component or fat atrophy of the vastus muscles. Additional plain computed tomography scan was also obtained that showed multiple poorly defined T2 hyperintense lobulated compartments with septations and multiple interspersed calcific foci which may represent phleboliths.On MR angiography, few dilated arterial branches from the deep femoral artery supplied the lesion with few areas of early enhancement without evidence of an early draining vein, definite venous shunting, or discrete nidus.Imaging features, phleboliths, and long-standing history of an intramuscular malformation in the young girl that was recalcitrant to treatment at previous attempts led us toward the suspicion of a FAVA rather than a vascular malformation or soft-tissue neoplasm and therefore a biopsy was recommended to confirmation.Pathology confirmed an intramuscular lesion with medium-sized arteries, veins, and lymphatics with intervening fibroadipose and muscle tissue. No evidence of atypia, necrosis, or malignancy was seen.Next-generation sequencing (NGS) was used for sequencing and variant/mutation detection, and a clinically relevant mutation was identified in the PIK3CA gene of the specimen clinching the diagnosis of FAVA.Keeping with the recommendations for management of FAVA as reported literature, the patient underwent a surgical excision relieving her of the contracture from the painful mass and restoring functional movement at the knee joint (Fig. 3).The patient will continue to remain under surveillance for recurrence of the lesion while she continues to rehabilitate for lower limb muscle and range of movement physical therapy.While we were unable to explain the limb shortening, we hypothesize that the paradoxical vasodilatory response associated with the vascular anomaly reduced the blood flow to the impaired perfusion area as seen with steal phenomena resulting in stunted affected limb growth.

Discussion

Initial working diagnosis before consultation at our institute was an arteriovenous malformation, considering it is the more common neoplasm [2]. However, we recognized the need for re-evaluation of the diagnosis in the presence of recalcitrant pain from an intramuscular neoplasm in the young girl, causing functional as well as growth limitations and failure to previous therapy [6].The diffuse mass appearance on imaging not amenable to endovascular embolization due to lack of nidus or specific feeding or draining vessels led to the evaluation for a distinct clinical entity in FAVA which was described in 2014 [1] and later revised in the ISSVA classification in 2018 [2, 7].Pathology is essential to confirm the diagnosis [8] and availability of NGS to identify a clinically relevant PIK3CA gene anomaly may be used to differentiate FAVA from close differentials of soft-tissue hamartomas or vascular malformation syndromes as was demonstrated in this case [5].Treatment options for FAVA differ from that of vascular malformations in the fact that surgical excision is the recommendation in literature as the curative option, especially in patients presenting with contractures and large mass lesions limiting the functional range of movement [4, 9].Near total excisions may be performed to limit the difficulty or disability resulting from large muscular resections due to the intramuscular tumor location [8]. Sirolimus has recently been used for treatment as an alternative to surgery in lesions in children under expert supervision [4]. Minimally invasive ablative procedures (radiofrequency/cryoablation) with or without embolization/sclerotherapy have been used with variable success aimed at pain relief rather than treatment of the lesion [7].

Conclusion

FAVA is a distinct and recently described entity posing a significant diagnostic and management challenge requiring a multidisciplinary team approach. While intramuscular lesions may lead to loss of muscle bulk with surgery, correction of deformities and resection of painful lesions improves function.

Clinical Message

FAVA is characterized by intramuscular replacement with fibro-fatty tissue, complex vascular malformation, phelbectesia, venous thrombosis, and lymphatic involvement. Considering the fact that FAVA is yet to be classified in the pathology classification of soft tissue neoplasms as a distinct entity, the diagnosis involves genetic mutation analysis for FAVA in a suspected combination of clinical, radiographic, and pathological neoplasm. Furthermore, the treatment of FAVA differs from its close differential in an intramuscular arteriovenous malformation.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 11-year-old girl.
- **Chief Complaint:** Painful swelling in the anterior right thigh.
- **History of Present Illness:** Painful small lump in the anterior thigh progressing over four years into a large painful swelling causing localized pain, limited knee movement, a limp, and pelvic/shoulder asymmetry.
- **Past Medical History:** Prior inconclusive incisional biopsy and failed sclerotherapy treatment.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Deep, tender intramuscular soft-tissue mass in anterior thigh muscles. Restricted knee range of motion (10°–40°). Calf muscle hypotrophy, >2 cm limb length discrepancy, and limb shortening.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - High-resolution ultrasound: Ill-defined hyperechoic area with vascular channels suggesting a vascular malformation.
  - MRI and MR angiography: Large heterogeneous intramuscular lesion (24 cm superoinferior length) in anterior thigh, sparing rectus femoris. Features included fat infiltration, T2 hyperintense lobulated areas, calcific foci (phleboliths), and dilated arterial supply with no nidus or venous shunting.
  - Pathology: Medium-sized arteries, veins, lymphatics with intervening fibrofatty and muscle tissue; no atypia or malignancy.
  - Next-generation sequencing (NGS): Confirmed PIK3CA gene mutation indicative of FAVA.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Arteriovenous Malformation (AVM):** Initially considered due to common frequency and vascular features; excluded based on absence of nidus, venous shunting, or definitive arterial or venous invasion on imaging.
2. **Soft-Tissue Neoplasm:** Pathology findings of fibrofatty and vascular components without atypia or malignancy excluded this as the primary diagnosis.
3. **Fibroadipose Vascular Anomaly (FAVA):** Supported by:
   - Pathologic findings revealing fibrofatty tissue with vascular and lymphatic involvement.
   - Imaging features consistent with vascular malformation and phleboliths.
   - Refractoriness to previous treatment.
   - Positive PIK3CA gene mutation confirmation.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Fibroadipose vascular anomaly (FAVA). Diagnosis based on clinical presentation, imaging suggesting intramuscular vascular malformation with phleboliths, histopathology findings, and genetic confirmation of a PIK3CA mutation.

---

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient is an 11-year-old girl presenting with a painful swelling in the anterior right thigh that has progressed over four years. This slow progression suggests a chronic process rather than acute infection or trauma. The symptoms include localized pain, limited knee movement, limp, and pelvic/shoulder asymmetry, indicating a significant and functionally impairing mass effect.

<step 2> **Consider Past Medical History:**  A prior inconclusive incisional biopsy and failed sclerotherapy are critical. The inconclusive biopsy suggests the initial diagnosis was challenging, and the lesion's nature is not straightforward. Failed sclerotherapy, typically used for vascular malformations, points towards a vascular component but potentially a more complex or resistant type of vascular lesion.

<step 3> **Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:**  The physical exam reveals a deep, tender intramuscular soft-tissue mass, confirming the location and nature of the swelling. Restricted knee range of motion (10°–40°) and limp are consistent with a mass in the thigh affecting joint mechanics. Calf muscle hypotrophy, limb length discrepancy (>2 cm), and limb shortening indicate chronic effects, potentially due to compression, altered growth, or chronic disuse. Pelvic/shoulder asymmetry is likely a compensatory postural change due to limb length inequality.

<step 4> **Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Ultrasound and MRI/MR Angiography:** Ultrasound suggests a vascular malformation, which is further supported by MRI/MR angiography findings. The MRI reveals a large (24 cm) heterogeneous intramuscular lesion with fat infiltration, T2 hyperintense lobulated areas, calcific foci (phleboliths), and dilated arterial supply without nidus or venous shunting. These features are characteristic of certain benign soft tissue lesions, particularly those with vascular and fatty components. The absence of a nidus and venous shunting helps to differentiate from classic arteriovenous malformations.

<step 5> **Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Pathology:** The pathology report describes medium-sized arteries, veins, lymphatics with intervening fibrofatty and muscle tissue, and no atypia or malignancy. This histopathological description reinforces the benign nature of the lesion and confirms its complex composition with vascular, lymphatic, fatty, and muscular elements.

<step 6> **Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS):** The NGS result is definitive: a confirmed PIK3CA gene mutation indicative of FAVA.  This genetic confirmation is crucial and strongly suggests the diagnosis. PIK3CA mutations are known to be associated with specific vascular and lymphatic malformations, and FAVA (Fibro-Adipose Vascular Anomaly) is specifically linked to these mutations.

<step 7> **Synthesize all Findings to reach a Final Diagnosis:** Combining all the clinical information – the slow-growing painful thigh mass, failed sclerotherapy, characteristic imaging findings (especially fat infiltration and phleboliths on MRI), benign pathology with vascular/lymphatic components, and the definitive PIK3CA mutation confirming FAVA – strongly points to **Fibro-Adipose Vascular Anomaly (FAVA)** as the final diagnosis. The patient's symptoms and all test results are consistent with this diagnosis.

### Answer:
Fibro-Adipose Vascular Anomaly (FAVA)

reasoning_eval.step: **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient is an 11-year-old girl presenting with a painful swelling in the anterior right thigh that has progressed over four years. This slow progression suggests a chronic process rather than acute infection or trauma. The symptoms include localized pain, limited knee movement, limp, and pelvic/shoulder asymmetry, indicating a significant and functionally impairing mass effect.

**Consider Past Medical History:**  A prior inconclusive incisional biopsy and failed sclerotherapy are critical. The inconclusive biopsy suggests the initial diagnosis was challenging, and the lesion's nature is not straightforward. Failed sclerotherapy, typically used for vascular malformations, points towards a vascular component but potentially a more complex or resistant type of vascular lesion.

**Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:**  The physical exam reveals a deep, tender intramuscular soft-tissue mass, confirming the location and nature of the swelling. Restricted knee range of motion (10°–40°) and limp are consistent with a mass in the thigh affecting joint mechanics. Calf muscle hypotrophy, limb length discrepancy (>2 cm), and limb shortening indicate chronic effects, potentially due to compression, altered growth, or chronic disuse. Pelvic/shoulder asymmetry is likely a compensatory postural change due to limb length inequality.

**Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Ultrasound and MRI/MR Angiography:** Ultrasound suggests a vascular malformation, which is further supported by MRI/MR angiography findings. The MRI reveals a large (24 cm) heterogeneous intramuscular lesion with fat infiltration, T2 hyperintense lobulated areas, calcific foci (phleboliths), and dilated arterial supply without nidus or venous shunting. These features are characteristic of certain benign soft tissue lesions, particularly those with vascular and fatty components. The absence of a nidus and venous shunting helps to differentiate from classic arteriovenous malformations.

**Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Pathology:** The pathology report describes medium-sized arteries, veins, lymphatics with intervening fibrofatty and muscle tissue, and no atypia or malignancy. This histopathological description reinforces the benign nature of the lesion and confirms its complex composition with vascular, lymphatic, fatty, and muscular elements.

**Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS):** The NGS result is definitive: a confirmed PIK3CA gene mutation indicative of FAVA.  This genetic confirmation is crucial and strongly suggests the diagnosis. PIK3CA mutations are known to be associated with specific vascular and lymphatic malformations, and FAVA (Fibro-Adipose Vascular Anomaly) is specifically linked to these mutations.

**Synthesize all Findings to reach a Final Diagnosis:** Combining all the clinical information – the slow-growing painful thigh mass, failed sclerotherapy, characteristic imaging findings (especially fat infiltration and phleboliths on MRI), benign pathology with vascular/lymphatic components, and the definitive PIK3CA mutation confirming FAVA – strongly points to **Fibro-Adipose Vascular Anomaly (FAVA)** as the final diagnosis. The patient's symptoms and all test results are consistent with this diagnosis.

<Step 2> Soft-Tissue Neoplasm excluded by pathology findings of fibrofatty and vascular components without atypia or malignancy.
